who_discriminated_against_you.other_who_discriminated_against_you: Property management company staff at Hudson View Properties, specifically the on-site property manager and assistant manager.

acts_of_discrimination.other_acts_of_discrimination:
I use a rollator walker due to multiple sclerosis and have a documented mobility impairment. When I signed my lease in June 2023, I specifically asked the property manager, Ms. Angela Cortez, about accessible parking and entry. She told me that my assigned parking spot would be in the underground garage near the elevator and that if I needed a closer spot as a disability accommodation, they would work with me.

After I moved in on 2023-07-01, I was assigned an outdoor parking spot at the far end of the lot, on an incline, about 300–350 feet from the main accessible entrance. I immediately requested, in writing on 2023-07-05, to be moved to one of the several unused spots next to the lobby entrance or to the garage level near the elevator, explaining my disability and attaching a copy of my doctor’s note. Over the next several months, I saw multiple other tenants—who did not appear to have mobility impairments and who moved in after me—assigned those closer spots. When I followed up with the assistant manager, he said, “Those spots are for long-term tenants and for people who don’t have all the special requests,” and laughed. I interpreted this as them minimizing and mocking my disability-related request.

In addition to the parking issue, the automatic door opener at the main entrance has been broken for most of my tenancy. I reported this first on 2023-08-10 through the online maintenance portal, then again on 2023-08-24 and 2023-09-12, emphasizing that I physically struggle to open the heavy glass door while managing my walker and groceries. Each time I was told it was “on the list.” As of 2024-11-15, the automatic opener still does not reliably function—sometimes it is turned off entirely—and staff have told me to “just ask someone in the lobby to help” instead of fixing it. I have fallen once (on 2024-03-02) trying to pull the door open during a windy day.

Regarding threats to evict me, after I continued to press for reasonable accommodations, including asking for written responses and mentioning that I might contact a fair housing agency, the tone from management changed dramatically. On 2024-04-18, I received a written notice taped to my door stating that they had received “multiple complaints” about me being “disruptive” and that any further “harassment of management staff” could result in non-renewal of my lease or early termination proceedings. The only so-called “harassment” I had engaged in was sending follow-up emails about my accommodation requests and asking for updates. When I spoke with Ms. Cortez about the notice, she said, in a raised voice, “If this place is so hard for you with your condition, maybe you should find somewhere else to live before your lease is up. We’re not required to keep someone who’s going to be a problem.” I perceived this as a direct threat to force me out because of my disability and my attempts to assert my rights.

I have also been denied equal use of common facilities. The building has a small fitness room and a community lounge on the first floor. On at least three occasions (2024-01-09, 2024-02-21, and 2024-06-04), I arrived at the lounge using my rollator and was told by the front desk staff that “management prefers” that I not use that space during busy hours because my walker “takes up too much room” and “people might trip.” I have seen other tenants host gatherings there without any issue. Additionally, the ramp leading to the rear entrance is often blocked by trash bins or delivery carts, and when I have complained, the superintendent has said, “You can still use the front stairs, it’s just a few steps,” even though I have repeatedly explained that stairs are extremely difficult and unsafe for me.

There have been several instances of harassing comments directly related to my disability. For example, on 2024-02-03, when I asked the superintendent why the elevator had been out of service for more than 24 hours without notice and mentioned that climbing five flights of stairs was not feasible for me, he responded, “Maybe a fifth-floor apartment in an older building isn’t the best place for someone in your condition. You should have thought about that before signing the lease.” Another time, on 2024-05-27, I overheard the assistant manager telling a maintenance worker, “She’s always complaining because of her ‘medical issues’—we’re not a hospital.” They stopped talking when they saw me in the hallway.

Overall, I believe I have been singled out and treated less favorably because of my disability. Management has refused to provide reasonable accommodations (like closer parking, timely repair of accessibility features, and unblocked ramps), has threatened my housing stability when I persisted in requesting those accommodations, and has allowed or engaged in comments and behavior that are dismissive and demeaning about my mobility impairment. This combination of denial of access, unequal treatment compared to other tenants, and explicit and implicit threats about my tenancy feels like a pattern of disability-based discrimination rather than isolated misunderstandings.
